blocks.content: Navigating Restrictive Corporate Networks

Enterprise environments route all external traffic through forward proxies (such as Squid or Zscaler) that perform SSL interception. To deploy agy-box reliably, you must configure the container runtime to trust corporate certificates and route image pull and push requests through the appropriate proxies.

Failing to inject corporate Root CA certificates into both the host container daemon and the agy-box image itself will result in immediate SSL handshake failures (x509: certificate signed by unknown authority) when communicating with public or private APIs.
Injecting Custom SSL Certificates
To ensure the engine can download dependencies and connect to LLM endpoints, the enterprise Root CA certificate must be copied into the container during the build stage.

# Custom Dockerfile extending the base agy-box image
FROM gcr.io/tridorian-corp/agy-box:v1.2.0

# Switch to root to import certificates
USER root

# Copy corporate ROOT CA certificate
COPY certs/corp-root-ca.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/corp-root-ca.crt

# Update certificates database
RUN chmod 644 /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/corp-root-ca.crt && \
    update-ca-certificates

# Revert to unprivileged runner user
USER agy_runner
Registry Setup & Proxy Configurations
Configure the container engine (Podman/Docker) to use the corporate proxy for pulling images, while bypassing the proxy for internal private registries.

# Create systemd override directory for Podman/Docker
sudo mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system/podman.service.d/

# Define proxy environment variables in systemd override configuration
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/podman.service.d/http-proxy.conf
[Service]
Environment="HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.corp.internal:8080"
Environment="HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.corp.internal:8080"
Environment="NO_PROXY=localhost,127.0.0.1,private-registry.corp.internal,169.254.169.254"
EOF

# Reload systemd daemon and restart the container service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart podman

# Authenticate to the private registry using corporate credentials
podman login private-registry.corp.internal \
  --username agy-deployer \
  --password-stdin < /etc/secrets/registry-token.txt
Google Chat App Setup
To enable the Google Chat MCP server to search and read messages, you must register a Google Chat app configuration in your GCP project:

items: Search for "Google Chat API" in the GCP Console search bar, and select Manage > Configuration.
Set the App Name field to "Chat MCP".
Set the Avatar URL field to "https://developers.google.com/chat/images/quickstart-app-avatar.png".
Set the Description field to "Chat MCP server".
Under Functionality, turn off "Enable interactive features" (as our agents run as background services).
Under Logs, select "Log errors to Logging" and click Save.

blocks.content: OAuth Consent branding and Granular Scopes
Google Workspace MCP servers require OAuth 2.0. In the Google Auth Platform portal, configure the OAuth Consent Screen and add the necessary scopes for each Workspace server:

items: Navigate to Google Auth Platform > Branding, and click Get Started.
Set App Name to "Google Workspace MCP Servers" and select your user support email.
Configure the Audience to Internal (restricts access to users in your organization) or External (requires adding your email under Test Users).
Under Contact Information, enter your developer email, then review and click Create.
Navigate to Data Access > Add or Remove Scopes, scroll to the bottom, and manually add the following scopes under "Manually add scopes":

blocks.content:
Google Calendar:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.calendarlist.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events.freebusy
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events.readonly

Google Chat:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.spaces.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.memberships.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.messages.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.users.readstate.readonly

Google Drive:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file

Gmail:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.compose

People API:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/directory.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.profile
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/contacts.readonly
Creating Web Application Client Credentials

With scopes registered, you must generate credentials. Since Antigravity interfaces coordinate the login flow on behalf of the developer, you must create a Web application client with an exact authorized redirect URI:

items: Go to Google Auth Platform > Clients > Create Client.
Select Web application as the application type.
Under Authorized redirect URIs, click "+ Add URI" and enter exactly: https://antigravity.google/oauth-callback
Click Create, and copy the generated Client ID and Client Secret to a secure configuration repository.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the exact redirect URI that must be registered under the Web application credentials for Antigravity Workspace MCP integration?
A) https://antigravity.google/oauth-callback
B) http://localhost:8080/callback
C) https://developers.google.com/oauth-callback
D) urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob
Correct Answer: A. The authorized redirect URI must be set exactly to https://antigravity.google/oauth-callback to align with Antigravity's OAuth flow.
Question 2: Which scope allows the Google Chat MCP server to search and read messages in space chats?
A) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.spaces.readonly
B) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.messages.readonly
C) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.bot
D) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.admin
Correct Answer: B. The https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.messages.readonly scope is required to search and read messages within space chats.
